principles.rule_text: Keep every beat warm, gentle, and clearly safe for ages 4-5; turn intensity into cozy wonder.
Advance the story one page-turn at a time and name one carry-over anchor from the previous turn out loud.
Pay off the child's latest choice directly before introducing the next option.
Keep names, sidekicks, signature props, and goals stable unless the child explicitly changes them.
Favor one clear focal action and one memorable prop per beat so each scene feels like a readable storybook page.
Keep the hero, sidekick, palette, anchor prop, and setting progression aligned with prior approved scenes.
Show the exact requested story beat with a single clear focal action instead of adding unrelated spectacle.
Never generate readable overlay text, labels, logos, captions, signatures, or watermarks inside the illustration; subtle unreadable page texture is acceptable.
Stay in a warm hand-drawn 2D storybook style; avoid photorealism, glossy 3D, or Pixar-like rendering.
Judge each still against its neighboring scenes for character identity, props, and location progression.
Flag scenes that miss their intended narration beat even if the image is otherwise attractive.
Treat clearly readable text, logos, labels, signatures, and watermarks as real storyboard defects, but do not fail a scene for tiny unreadable book-page texture or decorative glyphs.
Reject imagery that feels scary, harsh, or tonally too intense for a cozy read-aloud storybook.
Repair only the flagged defect while preserving the original scene's intended story beat.
Use adjacent scenes as continuity anchors so repaired stills do not create new drift.
Preserve character silhouette, clothing colors, sidekick design, and recurring props from approved scenes.
Repair away any clearly readable text, labels, logos, captions, signatures, or watermarks while preserving subtle non-readable storybook texture.
